resume:
VP, People & Talent

Summary
People executive with seventeen years across high-growth software, fintech, and a brief stint in industrial distribution. I have built and scaled HR functions from roughly two hundred employees through to a little over four thousand, across the United States, Canada, the UK, Ireland, India, and a small sales presence in Singapore. My remit has consistently spanned the full people lifecycle: talent acquisition, total rewards, HR business partnering, learning and organizational development, people analytics, employee relations, immigration, and the operational backbone of payroll and HRIS. I am comfortable as a strategic partner to a CEO and board, and equally comfortable sitting in a room walking a frontline manager through a difficult performance conversation. I care a great deal about building functions that are humane, fast, and legally sound, and I have learned the hard way that those three things are not in tension when the operating model is designed well.

Professional Experience

Nimbus Analytics — VP, People & Talent (2020 to present)
I joined Nimbus as the first executive-level people hire when the company was about eight hundred people and growing past a recent Series D. I inherited a recruiting team that was missing its hiring plan by a wide margin, a compensation philosophy that existed only in the founder's head, and an engagement survey that had never been run. Over four years I built the function out to a team of thirty-eight across talent acquisition, HRBP, total rewards, people operations, and a newly created people-analytics pod. I led the design and rollout of our first global leveling framework and compensation bands, which we benchmarked against a defined peer set and refreshed annually. I partnered with Finance to bring our annual merit and equity-refresh cycle onto a predictable calendar, and I introduced a manager-enablement curriculum because we were promoting strong individual contributors into management with almost no support. I also owned the people side of two acquisitions, including the integration of a forty-person team in Ireland, where I had to reconcile two very different benefits structures and a works-council expectation we had not planned for. During a difficult market in one of those years, I led a reduction in force that touched a meaningful portion of the org, and I designed the selection criteria, the severance framework, and the communication cadence in close partnership with Legal and the executive team. I consider how we handled the human dignity of that moment to be among the work I am most careful to get right.

Keystone Payments — Senior Director, HR Business Partnering (2016 to 2020)
At Keystone, a payments company of roughly two thousand six hundred employees, I led the HRBP organization supporting the engineering, product, and revenue functions. I rebuilt the performance-management approach away from a forced-ranking model that managers hated and toward a continuous-feedback model anchored on clear expectations and calibration. I led the people workstream for our expansion into India, standing up the Hyderabad site from a people, compliance, and talent-brand perspective, partnering with an employer-of-record initially and then transitioning to a direct entity. I was the senior people partner during a sensitive executive-misconduct investigation, working alongside outside counsel, and I learned to hold the line on process and confidentiality even under significant internal pressure. I also chaired our pay-equity review and drove remediation where the analysis surfaced unexplained gaps.

Gantry Software — HR Business Partner, then Manager (2012 to 2016)
I supported a fast-scaling sales and customer-success organization, partnering with leaders on workforce planning, org design, and the messier day-to-day of employee relations. I built the company's first onboarding program and its first manager-onboarding track. I handled a steady stream of employee-relations cases and developed the judgment to know when something was a coaching moment and when it needed a formal, documented process.

Meridian Industrial Supply — HR Generalist (2008 to 2012)
My first role out of school, in a distribution business with a large hourly and warehouse population. I ran benefits enrollment, leave administration, and a great deal of frontline employee relations. I learned wage-and-hour law in a very practical way, sat across from union stewards, and developed a respect for the operational reality of a workforce that clocks in and out.

Selected Themes and Capabilities
Organizational design and operating-model work: I have repeatedly taken functions that grew by accretion and redesigned them around clear ownership and service-level expectations. Total rewards: leveling frameworks, compensation banding, equity-refresh design, and benefits harmonization across geographies. Talent acquisition strategy: rebuilding sourcing engines, employer brand, and interview structures to reduce time-to-fill and improve quality-of-hire. People analytics: I am a believer in instrumenting the function so we can actually see attrition drivers, internal-mobility rates, and the health of the manager population. Employee relations and investigations: I have led and overseen sensitive matters with care and procedural rigor. Change management: acquisitions, integrations, reductions in force, and cultural integration after rapid hiring.

What I look for next
I am drawn to a VP or Chief People Officer mandate where the company is at an inflection point and needs someone who can both set people strategy at the board level and build the operating muscle underneath it. I want to work somewhere that treats the people function as a genuine driver of business outcomes rather than an administrative afterthought.

Education and Affiliations
BA, Industrial and Labor Relations, Cornell University. SHRM-SCP. Member of a regional CHRO peer roundtable. Occasional guest lecturer on people-analytics fundamentals at a local university's MBA program.
jd: We are searching for a VP of People to lead the global human-resources function for a 3,500-person enterprise software company preparing for a potential IPO within the next two years. Reporting to the CEO and partnering closely with the CFO and General Counsel, you will own talent acquisition, total rewards, HR business partnering, people operations, learning and development, and people analytics across the US, UK, India, and Germany. Immediate priorities include rebuilding a leveling and compensation framework that can withstand public-company scrutiny, reducing regretted attrition that currently sits near 18% annually, standing up a defensible pay-equity program, and professionalizing the manager population ahead of significant hiring. You will be a key voice on the compensation committee, will own the people-readiness workstream for IPO, and must have prior experience leading a workforce reduction humanely as well as overseeing sensitive employee-relations matters with outside counsel. This is a hands-on executive role for someone who can operate at the board level and in the details. Hybrid, with travel to international hubs roughly 25% of the time.

history.q: Give me your current scope at Nimbus — headcount supported, your team size, and what you own end to end.
You inherited recruiting missing its plan. What was the actual gap and what did you change first?
Walk me through how you built the global leveling framework. What peer set did you benchmark against?
Regretted attrition in this JD sits near 18%. At Nimbus, what was your number and what drove it?
Tell me about the manager-enablement curriculum — what specifically were managers failing at?
Let's go to the reduction in force. How did you build the selection criteria?
Who was in the room when the selection list was finalized, and who made the final call?
How did you partner with Legal to keep that RIF defensible — what was your process?
The Ireland integration and the works council — what surprised you and how did you adapt?
On the executive-misconduct investigation: how did you protect process under internal pressure?
Pay equity is called out in this role. Walk me through how you ran the analysis and remediated.
For an IPO-readiness people workstream, what are the first three things you'd stand up?
Back to the RIF: in the first 48 hours after notifications went out, what did you personally do to support the remaining managers?

candidate_last_answer: You know, what I always come back to is culture. Culture is the thing that carries you through these moments. I think a lot of companies underinvest in their employer brand, and honestly the war for talent is only getting harder — I was just reading about how Gen Z thinks about work differently, and I think we as leaders need to meet them where they are. We did a whole values-refresh exercise at Nimbus that I'm really proud of, we ran workshops, we got the executive team aligned on our cultural pillars, and that kind of foundational work is what makes everything else possible. So I really believe in leading with empathy and authenticity.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: We did a whole values-refresh exercise at Nimbus

expected-yield: pull the candidate off the culture/values tangent and back to the asked question — a concrete day-by-day account of the specific actions she personally took in the first 48 hours after the RIF notifications to support the remaining managers
